past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 6 years ago, well controlled on medication
Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 10 years ago, on stable levothyroxine replacement
Migraine without aura, episodic since her 30s, frequency 1-2 per month

medications.frequency: once daily in the morning on empty stomach

family_history: Mother had breast cancer diagnosed at age 62, now in remission
Father died of myocardial infarction at age 68
No known family history of brain tumors or neurological disease

social_history.occupation: High school English teacher, currently on medical leave for the past week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and 17-year-old daughter in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 3-4 times per week, has reduced activity over the past month due to fatigue

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Appears mildly anxious but cooperative. Attention grossly intact — performs serial sevens accurately (5 of 5). Language is fluent but notable for occasional word-finding pauses, particularly for low-frequency words. She circumlocutes when unable to retrieve the target word (e.g., says 'the thing you write with' instead of 'pen'). Repetition intact. Follows complex three-step commands. Reading and writing intact. MMSE 27/30 — loses 2 points on delayed recall (recalls 1 of 3 objects spontaneously, 1 with category cue, fails 1) and 1 point on naming (correctly names watch but calls stethoscope 'the doctor's listening thing').
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields: subtle right upper quadrantanopia on confrontation testing, confirmed with finger counting in the right upper field — patient consistently misses 2 of 5 stimuli. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No ptosis. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild right lower facial weakness noted on emotional expression but symmetric on volitional smile. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength full.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 5-/5 grip strength, 4+/5 finger abduction, 5/5 deltoid, biceps, and triceps. Right lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 throughout. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact bilaterally. Pinprick intact. Proprioception intact at fingers and toes. No sensory extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Left biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed on the right compared to the left but no frank dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. No meningismus. Mild difficulty with phonemic fluency (generates 8 words starting with 'F' in 60 seconds, below expected for education level). Semantic fluency relatively preserved (14 animals in 60 seconds).

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive word-finding difficulty and personality changes over 2 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Karen Whitfield is a 54-year-old right-handed woman and high school English teacher who presents with a two-month history of progressive difficulty finding words during her lectures, accompanied by subtle personality changes noticed primarily by her husband. Her husband reports that approximately two months ago, she began pausing more frequently during dinner conversations, searching for words that would normally come easily to her. As an English teacher known for her articulate speech and literary references, these pauses were immediately noticeable to her family and eventually to her colleagues. Over the past month, the word-finding difficulty has worsened, and she has started using circumlocutions — describing objects rather than naming them. Her husband also reports that she has become unusually irritable and short-tempered over the past six weeks, which is quite unlike her typically patient demeanor. She has had two episodes of unprovoked crying at work, leading to embarrassment and contributing to her decision to take medical leave one week ago. She describes a dull, persistent left-sided headache that developed approximately three weeks ago, which she initially attributed to stress and treated with ibuprofen. Unlike her typical migraines, this headache is constant rather than episodic, is not associated with photophobia or phonophobia, and only partially responds to her usual analgesics. She denies any nausea or vomiting. She has also noticed increasing fatigue and difficulty concentrating while grading papers, tasks she previously managed with ease. There has been no witnessed seizure activity, no episodes of loss of consciousness, and no visual disturbances that she has recognized, although her husband noted she has been bumping into doorframes on her right side more frequently over the past two weeks. She denies any fevers, weight loss, night sweats, or new skin lesions. She has no history of cancer and no known exposures to carcinogens. Her primary care physician initially attributed her symptoms to depression and work-related burnout, but after failing to improve on a short trial of sertraline and given the progressive nature of her symptoms, he ordered a brain MRI which revealed a left temporal lesion, prompting urgent referral and admission.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8-9 Hz alpha, reactive, symmetric over the right hemisphere but attenuated over the left temporal region
background.symmetry: Asymmetric — reduced amplitude and intermittent slowing over the left temporal region

findings.state: Present in both wakefulness and drowsiness, increases during hyperventilation
findings.clinical_correlation: Suggests underlying structural pathology in the left temporal region

findings.state: Brief runs lasting 3-8 seconds during wakefulness
findings.clinical_correlation: May represent peri-lesional cortical irritability or early subclinical seizure tendency

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, does not obscure underlying activity significantly

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Augments left temporal slowing; no definite electrographic seizure provoked
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response bilaterally; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating continuous regional left temporal slowing with intermittent semi-rhythmic delta activity. No definite epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures were captured during this 40-minute routine recording. The findings are consistent with an underlying structural lesion in the left temporal region. Clinical correlation with neuroimaging is recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording; prolonged or continuous EEG monitoring may be needed to capture epileptiform discharges if seizures are a clinical concern.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged EEG monitoring if seizure is suspected clinically
Consider prophylactic antiseizure medication given structural lesion and peri-lesional cortical irritability

findings.location: Left temporal lobe, centered in the posterior superior temporal gyrus extending into the middle temporal gyrus and insular cortex
findings.size: 3.8 x 3.2 x 2.9 cm in maximal dimensions

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous hypointensity with areas of intrinsic T1 shortening suggesting possible hemorrhage or proteinaceous material
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous hyperintensity with areas of low signal centrally
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense mass with extensive surrounding FLAIR signal abnormality extending into the temporal stem, posterior insula, and along white matter tracts toward the angular gyrus
signal_characteristics.DWI: Mildly restricted diffusion at the periphery of the mass suggesting moderate cellularity
signal_characteristics.Post-contrast: Irregular, predominantly solid enhancement pattern without classic central necrosis or ring enhancement. Enhancement is heterogeneous with some non-enhancing components

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the left temporal horn of the lateral ventricle. No significant midline shift (2 mm leftward falx deviation). Mild compression of the left ambient cistern.
findings.borders: Infiltrative margins with indistinct borders, particularly along the posterior extent where FLAIR signal blends with surrounding white matter edema

findings.location: Left temporal and parietal white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive hyperintensity extending beyond the enhancing tumor margin into temporal and inferior parietal white matter

findings.borders: Margins of edema extend to the left angular gyrus and along the external capsule

volumetrics.FLAIR_abnormality_volume: Approximately 52 mL including tumor and peritumoral edema

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions elsewhere in the brain parenchyma
No hydrocephalus beyond mild temporal horn effacement
Major intracranial vessels patent on MRA sequences
Left optic radiation fibers likely involved by the posterior extent of FLAIR abnormality, correlating with potential visual field deficit
mri.impression: 1. Left temporal lobe mass measuring 3.8 x 3.2 x 2.9 cm with irregular, heterogeneous enhancement and infiltrative margins. The enhancement pattern is atypical — predominantly solid without classic ring enhancement or central necrosis. 2. Extensive peritumoral FLAIR signal abnormality extending into the temporal stem and toward the angular gyrus. 3. Mild mass effect with early temporal horn effacement but no significant midline shift. 4. The imaging appearance is concerning for a high-grade glial neoplasm, although the absence of classic ring enhancement and necrosis raises the possibility of an anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO grade 3) or an atypical glioblastoma. CNS lymphoma and tumefactive demyelination should also be considered in the differential.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: High-grade glioma (glioblastoma, WHO grade 4 or anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO grade 3)
differential_by_imaging.key_features: Infiltrative mass with heterogeneous enhancement, peritumoral edema, mass effect, restricted diffusion at periphery suggesting cellularity
Can present with similar enhancement pattern; typically more homogeneous, periventricular, and associated with immunosuppression. This patient is immunocompetent.
Can present as large enhancing lesion but typically with open-ring enhancement, relatively less mass effect for size, and younger patient population. No MS history.
Typically more spherical with distinct margins at the gray-white junction. This mass has infiltrative borders inconsistent with metastasis. No known primary malignancy.

recommended_actions: MR perfusion imaging to evaluate relative cerebral blood volume in enhancing components
MR spectroscopy to characterize metabolite profiles
Consider stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection for tissue diagnosis
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out occult primary malignancy if metastasis remains in differential

Additional.clinical_significance: Within normal limits but at upper range; may be nonspecifically elevated in malignancy
Mildly elevated, may reflect inflammatory response to tumor or nonspecific finding

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely unremarkable with normal CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and hepatic and renal function — establishing a suitable baseline for potential surgical intervention and chemotherapy. CRP is mildly elevated at 4.8 mg/L, which may be a nonspecific inflammatory marker associated with the underlying neoplastic process. LDH is within normal limits but at the upper range. TSH is well controlled on levothyroxine. Overall, there are no laboratory contraindications to surgical management.

findings.location: Enhancing components of the left temporal mass, particularly the anterolateral solid portion

signal_characteristics.rCBV_ratio: Maximal rCBV ratio 3.8 (lesion-to-contralateral normal white matter), elevated above the 1.75 threshold for high-grade glioma
signal_characteristics.rCBV_map: Heterogeneous perfusion with focal hotspots of elevated rCBV corresponding to areas of solid enhancement
signal_characteristics.leakage_correction: K2 leakage correction applied; elevated rCBV values persist after correction

findings.location: Central non-enhancing component of the mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV_ratio: rCBV ratio 0.6, consistent with necrosis or cystic change despite absence of classic ring enhancement on conventional sequences

additional_observations: Peritumoral region shows rCBV ratio of 1.2-1.5, above normal white matter, suggesting possible tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin
No elevated perfusion elsewhere in the brain to suggest multifocal disease
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates significantly elevated rCBV (ratio 3.8) in the enhancing tumor components, strongly supportive of a high-grade neoplasm (WHO grade 3-4). A central low-perfusion zone suggests occult necrosis not apparent on conventional sequences, which would favor a glioblastoma (WHO grade 4) diagnosis despite the atypical enhancement pattern. Elevated peritumoral perfusion raises concern for tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing boundary.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: rCBV ratio >3.0 with central hypoperfusion suggesting necrosis strongly favors grade 4 glioma
Lymphoma typically shows elevated rCBV (ratio 2-3) but without central necrosis pattern

recommended_actions: Proceed with MR spectroscopy for further metabolic characterization
Tissue diagnosis remains essential — stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection recommended
Use perfusion maps for surgical planning to target highest rCBV areas for biopsy

findings.location: Left temporal mass, voxel placed in the solid enhancing component

signal_characteristics.choline: Markedly elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio 3.2, normal <1.5)
signal_characteristics.Cho_NAA_ratio: Cho/NAA ratio 8.0 (markedly elevated, consistent with high-grade neoplasm)
signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks at 1.3 ppm, consistent with necrosis and anaerobic metabolism
signal_characteristics.creatine: Mildly reduced creatine compared to contralateral normal tissue

findings.location: FLAIR-abnormal white matter adjacent to the enhancing mass, posterior temporal region

signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: No significant lipid or lactate peak

additional_observations: Spectroscopic pattern in the peritumoral region suggests tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin, consistent with perfusion findings
Contralateral temporal lobe shows normal metabolite ratios, serving as internal control
output.impression: MR spectroscopy of the left temporal mass demonstrates a metabolite profile highly consistent with a high-grade glial neoplasm: markedly elevated choline reflecting high membrane turnover, severely reduced NAA indicating neuronal loss/destruction, and prominent lipid-lactate peaks suggesting necrosis and anaerobic metabolism. The peritumoral spectroscopic abnormalities (elevated choline, reduced NAA without lipid-lactate) suggest tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing boundary, which is characteristic of infiltrative glioma rather than metastasis or lymphoma.
recommended_actions: Findings strongly support high-grade glioma — proceed with tissue diagnosis
Use spectroscopy maps in conjunction with perfusion for surgical planning
Peritumoral infiltration should be considered when planning resection margins

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Normal size and enhancement bilaterally, no masses
No pelvic masses or lymphadenopathy. Uterus and ovaries appear unremarkable.
No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions
Small 6 mm left thyroid nodule, likely benign
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.clinical_significance: Incidental finding, likely benign. Can follow up with thyroid ultrasound if clinically indicated.

output.interpretation: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast demonstrates no evidence of a primary malignancy or metastatic disease. There are no pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, adrenal masses, or suspicious lymphadenopathy. An incidental 6 mm left thyroid nodule is noted, likely benign. This negative metastatic workup supports a primary brain tumor rather than a metastatic lesion as the etiology of the intracranial mass.

Histopathology.clinical_significance: Confirms high-grade primary brain tumor with the most aggressive histologic subtype

Histopathology.value: Markedly hypercellular with pleomorphic astrocytic cells
Histopathology.clinical_significance: High cellularity consistent with aggressive tumor biology
High mitotic rate confirms high-grade malignancy
Diagnostic criterion for WHO grade 4 glioma

Molecular Markers.value: Wildtype (no mutation detected by immunohistochemistry and sequencing)
Molecular Markers.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype status confers worse prognosis compared to IDH-mutant gliomas
Unmethylated MGMT is associated with reduced benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy
Rules out oligodendroglioma; consistent with astrocytic lineage
TERT promoter mutation with IDH-wildtype status confirms molecular diagnosis of glioblastoma
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype GBM; potential therapeutic target
High proliferative index consistent with aggressive tumor biology

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy confirms the diagnosis of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4. Key histologic features include high cellularity, brisk mitotic activity, palisading necrosis, and microvascular proliferation. Molecular profiling reveals IDH-wildtype status, unmethylated MGMT promoter, TERT promoter mutation, and EGFR amplification. The unmethylated MGMT status suggests reduced benefit from temozolomide, although temozolomide remains part of the standard Stupp protocol. This molecular profile is consistent with primary (de novo) glioblastoma and carries a median overall survival of approximately 14-16 months with standard treatment.

signal_characteristics.Broca_area: Robust activation in the left inferior frontal gyrus (pars opercularis and triangularis), well anterior and superior to the tumor margin. Estimated distance from tumor margin: 2.8 cm.
signal_characteristics.Wernicke_area: Activation in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus is present but appears displaced posteriorly and superiorly by the mass. Estimated distance from enhancing tumor margin: 0.8 cm.
signal_characteristics.supplementary_motor_area: Normal activation in left medial frontal cortex, distant from tumor

findings.mass_effect: Tumor displaces Wernicke's area activation posteriorly

signal_characteristics.right_hand_motor: Robust activation in the left precentral gyrus hand knob, distant from tumor. Estimated distance from tumor margin: 3.5 cm.
signal_characteristics.face_motor: Left precentral gyrus face area activation, superior to tumor. Estimated distance: 2.1 cm.

additional_observations: White matter tractography (DTI): left arcuate fasciculus is displaced inferiorly and medially by the mass but remains intact. The tract courses along the inferior margin of the tumor.
Left inferior longitudinal fasciculus is partially infiltrated by FLAIR-abnormal tissue
Corticospinal tract is distant from the tumor and not at risk
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates strong left hemisphere language dominance. Broca's area activation is well separated from the tumor (2.8 cm). However, Wernicke's area activation is in close proximity to the posterior enhancing tumor margin (0.8 cm), creating significant risk for language deficit during posterior resection. The left arcuate fasciculus is displaced but intact along the inferior tumor margin. Motor cortex activation is distant from the tumor and not at surgical risk. These findings indicate that maximal safe resection will be limited by the posterior tumor margin's proximity to Wernicke's area, and awake craniotomy with intraoperative language mapping should be strongly considered.
recommended_actions: Awake craniotomy with intraoperative cortical and subcortical language mapping is recommended
Intraoperative neuronavigation should integrate fMRI and DTI data
Discuss with patient the risk of postoperative language deficit given Wernicke's area proximity
Consider subtotal resection of the posterior component to preserve language function

output.query: glioblastoma IDH-wildtype MGMT unmethylated treatment standard of care Stupp protocol 2024

results.title: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.key_finding: The Stupp protocol (maximal safe resection followed by concurrent chemoradiation with temozolomide and 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide) improved median overall survival from 12.1 to 14.6 months compared to radiation alone.

results.title: Effect of Tumor-Treating Fields Plus Maintenance Temozolomide vs Maintenance Temozolomide Alone on Survival in Patients With Glioblastoma (EF-14 trial)
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.key_finding: Addition of tumor-treating fields (TTFields) to maintenance temozolomide improved median overall survival from 16.0 to 20.9 months in newly diagnosed GBM.

results.title: MGMT promoter methylation status as a prognostic and predictive biomarker in glioblastoma
results.authors: Hegi ME, Diserens AC, Gorlia T, et al.
results.key_finding: MGMT promoter methylation is associated with improved survival with temozolomide. Unmethylated patients derive less benefit from temozolomide but it remains part of standard care as no superior alternative exists.

output.summary: The standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma remains the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by 6 weeks of concurrent radiation (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m2), followed by 6-12 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2, days 1-5 of 28-day cycle). Tumor-treating fields (TTFields, Optune) may be added during the maintenance phase based on the EF-14 trial. For MGMT-unmethylated tumors, the benefit of temozolomide is reduced but it remains standard due to the absence of a clearly superior alternative. Clinical trial enrollment should be actively considered, particularly for MGMT-unmethylated patients. Extent of resection is a significant prognostic factor, but must be balanced against preservation of neurological function in eloquent cortex tumors.

interactions: Ibuprofen: Increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and peptic ulcer disease with concurrent corticosteroid and NSAID use. Discontinue ibuprofen or add gastroprotection.
Levothyroxine: Corticosteroids may decrease TSH secretion and alter thyroid hormone metabolism. Monitor TSH levels during prolonged dexamethasone use.
warnings: Hyperglycemia: Dexamethasone frequently causes steroid-induced hyperglycemia. Monitor blood glucose regularly, especially given the patient's BMI of 26.8.

Immunosuppression: Prolonged corticosteroid use increases infection risk. Pneumocystis prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) should be considered if dexamethasone is continued for >6 weeks, noting penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity with TMP-SMX is very low).
Myopathy: Prolonged dexamethasone use may cause steroid myopathy, which can be difficult to distinguish from tumor-related weakness.
Taper: Gradual taper required when discontinuing to avoid adrenal crisis.
alternatives: Methylprednisolone (if oral dexamethasone is not tolerated)
Bevacizumab (as steroid-sparing agent for refractory cerebral edema)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4, left temporal lobe

differential.key_distinguishing: Infiltrative borders and peritumoral spectroscopic abnormalities are inconsistent with metastasis. Negative systemic staging workup. Metastases are typically more spherical with distinct margins.
Patient is immunocompetent. Enhancement pattern is heterogeneous rather than the homogeneous enhancement typical of lymphoma. Lymphoma typically responds dramatically to corticosteroids.

Absence of classic ring enhancement and necrosis on conventional MRI could suggest grade 3; however, perfusion showing central hypoperfusion (occult necrosis) and spectroscopy with lipid-lactate peaks favor grade 4. Biopsy is definitive.

No history of multiple sclerosis. Enhancement pattern is not the typical open-ring seen in demyelination. Spectroscopy shows elevated choline and lipid-lactate rather than the lactate-only peaks of demyelination.
Presence of enhancement, elevated perfusion (rCBV 3.8), and markedly elevated Cho/NAA ratio exclude low-grade histology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history focusing on timeline and progression of word-finding difficulty, personality changes, and new headache pattern. Distinguish from typical migraines.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive symptoms over 2 months, headache atypical for her usual migraines, personality change and word-finding difficulty suggesting left temporal lobe pathology

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including detailed language assessment, visual field testing, and cognitive screening
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Word-finding difficulty, right upper quadrantanopia, mild right hand weakness, reduced phonemic fluency — all localizing to left temporal region

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast including DWI sequences
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal irregular enhancing mass with infiltrative margins, peritumoral edema, mild mass effect

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, liver function tests, and LDH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs establishing baseline for surgical and chemotherapy planning; mildly elevated CRP

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG given risk of seizures with cortical mass
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal regional slowing consistent with structural lesion; no definite epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone for peritumoral edema management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symptomatic improvement in headache and potentially cognitive symptoms as edema decreases

optimal_actions.action: Order advanced MRI including MR perfusion and MR spectroscopy to better characterize the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV and elevated choline with lipid-lactate peaks, strongly supporting high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out occult primary malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of systemic malignancy, supporting primary brain tumor diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Request neurosurgical consultation for biopsy and/or resection planning. Order functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping given tumor proximity to eloquent cortex.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Wernicke's area displaced but in close proximity to posterior tumor margin (0.8 cm), necessitating awake craniotomy with intraoperative mapping

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with maximal safe resection via awake craniotomy with intraoperative language mapping, followed by stereotactic biopsy of residual tumor
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Histopathologic confirmation of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4

optimal_actions.action: Initiate Stupp protocol: concurrent radiation (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide, followed by adjuvant temozolomide cycles. Discuss tumor-treating fields (TTFields).
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard of care treatment initiated despite unmethylated MGMT

optimal_actions.action: Initiate prophylactic antiseizure medication (levetiracetam) given structural lesion with EEG evidence of cortical irritability
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure prophylaxis in the perioperative period

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neuro-oncology for clinical trial evaluation given MGMT-unmethylated status and EGFR amplification
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Potential enrollment in trials targeting EGFR-amplified GBM or MGMT-unmethylated tumors

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the lesion and assess for mass effect
Recognize the progressive word-finding difficulty and personality change as symptoms localizing to the left temporal lobe rather than attributing them to depression or burnout
Initiate dexamethasone for peritumoral edema to prevent clinical deterioration while workup proceeds
Perform comprehensive metastatic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) to distinguish primary brain tumor from metastasis
Obtain advanced MRI (perfusion and spectroscopy) to better characterize the atypical enhancement pattern and differentiate high-grade glioma from lymphoma or demyelination
Order functional MRI before surgery given tumor proximity to language-eloquent cortex in the dominant hemisphere
Obtain tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling including IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, and TERT status to guide treatment decisions
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing progressive word-finding difficulty and personality changes as depression without neuroimaging — a progressive focal neurological syndrome demands imaging
Performing open biopsy or resection without pre-surgical functional mapping in a tumor adjacent to Wernicke's area
Administering whole-brain radiation as initial treatment — focal radiation with the Stupp protocol is standard for newly diagnosed GBM
Starting empiric antibiotics for suspected brain abscess without imaging evidence — this lesion lacks the characteristic restricted diffusion centrally and thin smooth ring enhancement of abscess
Withholding corticosteroids in a symptomatic patient with significant peritumoral edema and mass effect
Performing lumbar puncture with a mass lesion and evidence of early temporal horn effacement without first ensuring it is safe (risk of herniation)
key_reasoning_points: Progressive word-finding difficulty in a previously articulate individual, combined with personality change and new-onset non-migrainous headache, constitutes a progressive focal neurological syndrome requiring urgent neuroimaging

The atypical MRI enhancement pattern (irregular solid enhancement without classic ring enhancement or necrosis) complicates the imaging differential — advanced MRI (perfusion and spectroscopy) is essential to characterize the lesion further
MR perfusion showing elevated rCBV with a central low-perfusion zone reveals occult necrosis not visible on conventional sequences, effectively upgrading the imaging impression to WHO grade 4

MR spectroscopy demonstrating elevated Cho/NAA ratio with lipid-lactate peaks in the mass, plus elevated choline in the peritumoral region, supports high-grade infiltrating glioma and helps distinguish from lymphoma or demyelination
Negative systemic staging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) supports a primary brain tumor rather than metastasis, which is important given the solitary nature of the lesion

Functional MRI is critical for surgical planning in dominant hemisphere temporal lobe tumors — proximity of Wernicke's area to the posterior tumor margin (0.8 cm) necessitates awake craniotomy with intraoperative mapping to maximize resection while preserving language

MGMT promoter methylation status, while not changing the decision to use temozolomide (still standard of care), has prognostic implications and should prompt consideration of clinical trial enrollment for unmethylated patients
The subtle right upper quadrantanopia on exam correlates with involvement of the left optic radiation fibers, providing additional localizing value and consistent cross-modal findings

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate — 54-year-old woman with progressive word-finding difficulty and personality change. MRI shows an irregular left temporal enhancing mass without classic ring enhancement or necrosis, requiring advanced imaging (perfusion and spectroscopy) to confirm high-grade etiology and differentiate from other enhancing lesions.
clinical_pearls: Progressive word-finding difficulty in a dominant hemisphere lesion may initially be misattributed to depression, stress, or early dementia — neuroimaging should be obtained for any progressive focal cognitive deficit

Not all glioblastomas present with classic ring enhancement and central necrosis — advanced MRI techniques (perfusion and spectroscopy) can reveal high-grade features not apparent on conventional sequences
Elevated rCBV in the peritumoral FLAIR-abnormal region suggests tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin, a hallmark of infiltrative gliomas that distinguishes them from metastases
Functional MRI and DTI tractography are essential for pre-surgical planning when tumors involve or are adjacent to eloquent cortex, particularly language areas in the dominant hemisphere
